title: Lock refresh can overwrite a newly reclaimed holder
detail: refreshLoopLock() reads the current holder, checks only ownerPid, then blindly rewrites the lock with writeLoopLockAtomic(). A stale reclaimer can concurrently move the old lock aside and create a new exclusive lock. If the old owner read before reclaim and refreshes after the new owner acquired, its rename overwrites the new lock. The CLI refresh path passes only ownerPid, so there is no acquisition nonce or generation to make refresh conditional.
claim: A delayed heartbeat from a stale-but-still-running owner can resurrect that owner over a newer claimant, allowing two loop hosts to believe they own the same packet lock.
counterevidenceSought: Checked the acquire/reclaim path and CLI adapter for a lock identity token beyond PID; none is persisted or passed through refresh. Host-local single-flight is optional and the CLI acquire path calls acquireLoopLock() without it.
alternativeExplanation: The workflow may normally kill or abandon a stale owner before another process reclaims the lock.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade only if workflow evidence proves a process whose heartbeat exceeded the stale threshold cannot ever resume and call refreshLoopLock().
suggestedFix: Add an acquisition nonce/generation to LoopLockData, return it to the holder, and require refresh/release to prove the same lock identity under a compare-and-swap style critical section; alternatively serialize refresh and reclaim through the same writer lock.

title: Injection clamping can remove the directive and closing marker
detail: `renderGoalInjection()` accepts up to a 2000-character objective and 280-character verifier reason, then clamps the entire rendered block to the 2400-character injection cap. With accepted max-length fields, the tail is truncated before the directive and `[/active_goal]`, so `mk_goal_status` can return an exact injection preview that is not the documented bracketed active-goal block.
claim: Accepted objective/reason lengths can produce a malformed active-goal injection that omits both the directive and closing marker.
counterevidenceSought: Checked goal tests for worst-case injection preview assertions; they assert the opening marker only and do not require the directive or closing marker to survive clamping.
alternativeExplanation: The hard cap may intentionally trump well-formedness, but then the command/tool contract should not describe an exact bracketed block with a directive.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if the injection contract is changed to explicitly allow truncated, non-closed blocks and tests prove no consumer depends on directive or closing marker shape.
suggestedFix: Reserve room for the injection skeleton before clamping dynamic fields; clamp objective and reason to derived budgets, then always append usage, directive, and closing marker. Add boundary tests for max field lengths and low maxInjectionChars.

title: Rejected prompt events clear the autonomous continuation blocker
detail: The handler sets blockedByPrompt on permission.asked/question.asked, and maybeContinueGoal suppresses continuation while that guard is present. But question.rejected is grouped with successful replies and clears both the volatile session block and persisted blockedByPrompt flag, so the next idle event can pass the prompt-block gate and dispatch promptAsync after a user rejection.
claim: Rejected prompt actions can clear the only prompt-block guard and allow autonomous continuation after user rejection.
counterevidenceSought: Checked for a later rejection-specific guard, suppression write, or unit test after question.rejected; the code only deletes the volatile block and writes blockedByPrompt=false, while tests only cover permission.asked setting the block.
alternativeExplanation: If OpenCode uses question.rejected to mean a harmless resolved prompt rather than user denial, clearing may be intentional. The event name and fail-closed autonomy requirement point the other way.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if runtime documentation proves question.rejected is emitted only for non-user-denial cleanup events and cannot follow a user rejection that should halt continuation.
suggestedFix: Distinguish accepted replies from rejected or denied replies. Keep blockedByPrompt true, or set a suppression/block reason, when a question or permission is rejected so autonomy requires an explicit user reset before continuing.

title: setGoal can revive stale terminal state and carry budgets into replacement goals
detail: setGoal computes tokenBudget from current?.tokenBudget before branching, then the same-objective branch spreads the current record while forcing status back to active. After a budget_limited or complete goal, /goal set with the same objective preserves stale tokensUsed, tokenBudget, verifier, continuation, and completion fields; a different replacement objective also inherits the old budget when tokenBudget is omitted.
claim: A normal /goal set can produce an active goal that still carries terminal-state accounting or an unintended previous budget, causing immediate budget stops or stale verifier/completion state.
counterevidenceSought: I checked whether the command or tests reset terminal fields or pass an explicit null tokenBudget on replacement; goal.md only passes objective for set, and the state tests cover active same-objective idempotence but not terminal reactivation.
alternativeExplanation: The budget inheritance may have been intended as a sticky session setting, but the same-objective terminal reactivation still leaves active records with terminal counters and stale verifier metadata.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if the product contract explicitly says /goal set same-objective is only an idempotent resume of the exact same goal, including preserving exhausted budgets and verifier state, and users must /goal clear before restarting.
suggestedFix: Create a fresh goal when current.status is not active, reset usage/verifier/completion/continuation fields when reactivating, and only inherit tokenBudget for an active same-objective update or when the caller explicitly asks for inheritance.

title: Same-objective set can reactivate exhausted goal state
detail: `/goal set` routes normal user input to `mk_goal` set, but `setGoal()` special-cases an identical objective by spreading the existing record, changing `status` back to `active`, and clearing continuation suppression without resetting usage, verifier, completion, continuation, or activity fields. A budget-limited goal can therefore become active again while `tokensUsed` is still equal to `tokenBudget`, and the active injection advertises stale exhausted usage.
claim: Setting the same objective after a terminal or budget-limited state violates the set/replace contract and can inject stale active goal state.
counterevidenceSought: Looked for docs or tests declaring same-objective set to be a resume/idempotence operation across terminal states; only active same-objective identity preservation is tested.
alternativeExplanation: Preserving the current record may be intended for repeated active set calls, but that does not explain reactivating an exhausted or terminal record while keeping stale terminal counters.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade only if the product contract explicitly says same-objective set resumes terminal/budgeted goals with all counters intact and tests pin the active-but-exhausted injection as expected.
suggestedFix: Make set always replace/reset the record, or allow same-objective no-op only for already-active, non-exhausted goals; terminal or budget-limited records should use the fresh-goal path or reset usage/verifier/completion/continuation/activity fields consistently.

title: Usage dedupe only remembers the immediately previous message
detail: accountUsage skips a message only when messageID equals current.lastAccountedMessageID, then overwrites that field with the latest charged id. The lifecycle test proves adjacent replay is skipped, but an interleaved A/B/A message.updated order charges A twice and can prematurely trip budget_limited.
claim: Repeated message.updated events are not reliably deduped unless duplicates are contiguous, so token accounting can be silently inflated in normal streaming or delayed-update scenarios.
counterevidenceSought: I looked for a stronger OpenCode ordering guarantee or a test covering interleaved duplicate updates; the local lifecycle spec explicitly calls out repeated message.updated events, and the test only replays the same message immediately.
alternativeExplanation: If OpenCode guarantees a session can never emit a duplicate message.updated after any other message id has been observed, the single-slot dedupe is sufficient.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if runtime documentation or live tracing proves duplicate message.updated events are always contiguous and message ids are always present for usage-bearing events.
suggestedFix: Persist a bounded set of accounted message ids per goal, or store per-message cumulative totals and add only deltas; skip budget accounting for usage-bearing events without a stable message id.

title: Read-side JSONL repair ignores the writer lock
detail: appendRoundStateRecord() acquires a lock before repair/write, but readRoundStateRecords() repairs by default without acquiring or checking that lock. A concurrent reader can see a partial trailing line and truncate the state file while the writer still owns the cooperative lock.
claim: The read path can mutate round-state JSONL without participating in the single-writer lock protocol, creating a data-loss race with active appends.
counterevidenceSought: Looked for read-side lock acquisition or a caller guarantee that readRoundStateRecords() is only invoked after writers finish; the module default remains repair-on-read and the exported reader has no lock path.
alternativeExplanation: If all current readers run strictly after orchestration completion, the race is latent until resume/monitoring overlaps with a write. The module API itself does not enforce that ordering.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if all production callers are proven single-process and non-overlapping, or if regular-file append visibility is otherwise wrapped by an external process lock.
suggestedFix: Do not truncate from readRoundStateRecords() unless the reader acquires the same lock, or make repair false by default for reads and expose a separate locked repair operation for resume recovery.

title: Round-state append corrupts a valid final JSONL line without a newline
detail: repairRoundStateJsonl() treats a trailing valid JSON object without a newline as clean. appendRoundStateRecord() then appends the next JSON object immediately after it, producing a concatenated invalid line; the next repair pass can truncate back to the prior newline and drop records.
claim: A state file ending with a valid JSON record but no trailing newline is considered unrepaired, and the next append corrupts the JSONL stream.
counterevidenceSought: Checked round-state tests; they cover clean files with a trailing newline and invalid partial tails, but not a valid no-newline tail. The append code does not add a separator based on current file content.
alternativeExplanation: If the state writer contract guarantees every durable record always includes the newline, this is a recovery-edge bug rather than steady-state corruption; crash/manual recovery can still leave this exact tail shape.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if a lower layer proves append writes and crash recovery can never leave a complete JSON object without its trailing newline.
suggestedFix: Under the append lock, ensure a non-empty file ends with newline before appending, or make repair normalize a valid no-newline tail by preserving it and adding the separator. Add a two-record regression for this case.

title: Review FILE coverage gaps use the wrong edge direction
detail: findCoverageGaps() includes FILE nodes in the review target set, but then treats every review target as covered only by outgoing COVERS or EVIDENCE_FOR edges from the node. The review seed path emits SLICE -> FILE COVERS edges, and hotspot/scoring code also treats files as covered when the file is the edge target, so query.cjs --query-type coverage_gaps can report covered files as gaps.
claim: A normal review graph with a seeded SLICE/DIMENSION -> FILE COVERS edge can still report that FILE as uncovered because findCoverageGaps checks e.source_id = n.id for FILE nodes.
counterevidenceSought: Checked the review seed transform, convergence scoring, hotspot saturation, and query.cjs exposure. I did not find a producer that converts seeded file coverage into outgoing FILE edges.
alternativeExplanation: If FILE nodes are intended to be producers rather than covered targets, the seed and scoring paths are the inconsistent side; either way the live query/scoring contract is split.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if the review graph contract is amended to state that FILE coverage gaps intentionally require outgoing FILE -> evidence/finding edges and seeded SLICE -> FILE edges are not meant to satisfy coverage_gaps.
suggestedFix: Split review gap logic by kind. Treat FILE nodes as covered by inbound COVERS from a slice/dimension and/or inbound IN_FILE, then add an integration test with a SLICE/DIMENSION -> FILE edge that must not produce a FILE gap.

title: Coverage graph metadata is accepted and re-emitted without redaction
detail: upsert.cjs copies arbitrary node/edge metadata into graph rows, the DB serializes it wholesale, and coverage query helpers parse and return the full metadata object to query.cjs responses. The sibling council query surface has an explicit sanitizeMetadata allowlist, which confirms the expected prompt-safe boundary exists elsewhere but is missing for coverage graphs. A normal graph event carrying token, api_key, copied .env text, or credentials in metadata will be persisted and emitted into query stdout for downstream prompts/logs.
claim: Coverage graph query output can leak secrets from arbitrary metadata.
counterevidenceSought: Searched for a coverage-graph metadata sanitizer and found only council-scoped sanitization; coverage query branches return raw parsed metadata.
alternativeExplanation: Producers may intend metadata to contain only non-sensitive fields, but that contract is not enforced by upsert.cjs or the DB/query layer.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if all coverage graph producers validate metadata against a non-sensitive schema before invoking upsert.cjs and query output is never sent to prompts or logs.
suggestedFix: Add a coverage-graph prompt-safe projection that allowlists known scalar metadata keys and redacts common secret patterns before query output; keep full metadata only in storage if needed, or reject sensitive keys at upsert time.

title: Research claim verification query disagrees with convergence scoring
detail: computeResearchClaimVerificationRateFromData() counts any present verification_status other than unresolved as verified, but findUnverifiedClaims() only accepts literal verified. Claims with statuses like supported or confirmed can pass the convergence score while still appearing in unverified_claims query output and warning blockers.
claim: The query and signal paths use different verified-claim predicates, so the same CLAIM row can be both verified for claimVerificationRate and unverified for findUnverifiedClaims.
counterevidenceSought: Searched for a documented finite status enum or adapter that normalizes all verified statuses to literal verified before query time; found only the signal-side contract that non-unresolved is verified.
alternativeExplanation: If only verified and unresolved are valid statuses in practice, this is latent until another accepted status appears, but the signal code explicitly accepts the broader contract.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if producers are constrained by schema/tests to emit only verification_status values verified or unresolved and the signal comment is corrected to that narrower enum.
suggestedFix: Extract one shared isClaimVerified predicate and use it in both query and signal code. Add a test for a non-unresolved status across claimVerificationRate and unverified_claims.

title: writeStateAtomic can write invalid JSON for non-representable top-level state
detail: writeStateAtomic interpolates JSON.stringify(data, null, 2) directly. For top-level values such as undefined, functions, or symbols, JSON.stringify returns undefined instead of a JSON string, so the function writes the literal body undefined\n and leaves the state file unparsable.
claim: A bad or missing state snapshot can be persisted as invalid JSON instead of failing closed before touching the target file.
counterevidenceSought: Looked for a guard or test covering undefined/function/symbol top-level state; the file has serializePrettyState() with the needed guard, but writeStateAtomic() bypasses it and tests cover object payloads only.
alternativeExplanation: Callers may intend to pass only JSON objects, but the public function accepts unknown and documents a serialization throw path, so this should still fail before writing corrupt state.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if all production callers are proven by type/schema to pass only object snapshots and writeStateAtomic is made private to those callers.
suggestedFix: Use serializePrettyState(data) inside writeStateAtomic(), or explicitly throw when JSON.stringify does not return a string before calling writeTextAtomic().

title: Diff-gated JSONL append can lose concurrent rows
detail: appendJsonlIfChangedAtomic() reads the entire target file, builds a new whole-file payload, then replaces the file with writeTextAtomic(). There is no lock or reread inside a critical section. Two processes appending different rows can both read the same prefix, pass the fingerprint checks, and then the last rename wins, dropping the other row. The helper is used by command subprocesses for the status ledger.
claim: The append helper is atomic as a replace, not atomic as an append; concurrent writers can silently lose a valid JSONL row.
counterevidenceSought: Checked the unit coverage around atomic-state.vitest.ts:155; it verifies duplicate suppression and changed-row appends in one process, but not concurrent writers.
alternativeExplanation: Current workflow callers may be serialized by an outer loop lock, making the helper effectively single-writer in deployed paths.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if every current and intended caller is proven to hold a mutually exclusive lock for the target ledger before calling this helper, and the helper is documented as single-writer only.
suggestedFix: Put the read-last-fingerprint and append under the shared writer lock, or switch to true append semantics with O_APPEND plus a locked duplicate check. Add a two-process test where both writers append distinct rows to the same ledger.

title: Deferred writer timer path can create unhandled write rejections
detail: scheduleFlush() starts the async drain with void drainPendingWrites(). If writeTextAtomic throws during that timer-driven drain, the returned promise rejects without an observer. Callers that only invoked write() cannot catch the filesystem error, and Node can report or terminate on an unhandled rejection.
claim: Background persistence failures in the debounced writer escape the runtime's error taxonomy and can crash or disappear instead of being reported through flushNow()/close().
counterevidenceSought: Checked the writer tests and found coalescing/flush/close coverage, but no timer-path filesystem failure case and no stored last-error/error callback in the implementation.
alternativeExplanation: A caller that always immediately awaits flushNow() or close() may observe errors, but the default debounce path is explicitly timer-driven and write() returns void.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if the API contract is changed so timer-driven flushes are best-effort telemetry only and every durable caller is required to await flushNow()/close() before relying on the file.
suggestedFix: Attach a catch handler to timer-driven drains, retain the failure, and make flushNow()/close() reject with the retained error; optionally add an onError callback for callers that never flush manually.

title: Deferred writer timer flush can create an unhandled rejection
detail: `write()` schedules a debounce timer, and the timer callback invokes `void drainPendingWrites()` without a catch or stored error state. The async drain calls `writeTextAtomic()`, which rethrows write/fsync/rename failures. A directory target, permissions error, ENOSPC, or fsync failure on the timer-driven path therefore rejects a promise nobody observes. The tests cover direct atomic cleanup failure and deferred success paths, but not automatic timer-flush failure or unhandledRejection behavior.
claim: The normal debounce-driven flush path drops write failures on the floor as unhandled promise rejections, which can crash the process or hide a failed state write.
counterevidenceSought: Checked atomic-state tests for a deferred-writer failure path or process-level unhandledRejection assertion; the existing failure test targets direct writeStateAtomic only, while deferred tests all use successful writes/flushes.
alternativeExplanation: Callers may usually close or flush during orderly shutdown, where errors are awaitable. That does not cover the documented default debounce behavior, where write() returns void and the timer is the normal flush mechanism.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if runtime callers are changed so the timer path is never used, or if the writer installs a tested error sink that makes timer failures observable and non-crashing.
suggestedFix: Catch timer-drain failures, store the last error on the writer, and make flushNow()/close() rethrow it; optionally expose an onError hook for background flush failures. Add a Vitest case that targets a directory or mocked failing write through the debounce timer and asserts no unhandled rejection plus observable failure state.

title: Locked JSONL merge can overwrite unlocked appends
detail: appendJsonlRecord() writes directly with appendFileSync() and does not acquire the writer lock. mergeJsonlUnderLock() locks only merge writers, reads the current file, and rewrites the whole JSONL file via temp+rename. If a plain append lands after the merge read and before the rename, the merge snapshot omits that appended row. This can affect state logs because salvage uses mergeJsonlUnderLock() while audit and validation paths use appendJsonlRecord().
claim: A state-log event can be silently lost when a locked merge races an unlocked append to the same JSONL file.
counterevidenceSought: Checked current JSONL tests; they cover concurrent merge writers and direct appends separately, but not mergeJsonlUnderLock racing appendJsonlRecord on the same file.
alternativeExplanation: The orchestrator may serialize salvage and dispatch-validation/audit append paths so these helpers are never concurrent for one state log.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if workflow evidence proves mergeJsonlUnderLock() and appendJsonlRecord() cannot run concurrently against the same JSONL path.
suggestedFix: Make all JSONL writers for a path share the same writer lock, or change merge to an append-only strategy. Otherwise enforce a contract that appendJsonlRecord() is not used on files that may also be merged under lock.

title: Corrupt lock files become unreclaimable and misreport the contender as holder
detail: readLoopLock() returns null for parse/read failures. When an existing empty or malformed lock file is present, acquireLoopLockFileOnly() goes down the no-holder branch, writeLoopLockExclusive() returns false on EEXIST, rereads null, and returns acquired:false with current ?? lock. That prevents stale reclaim and reports the would-be acquirer's own lock data as the holder.
claim: A crash that leaves an empty or malformed lock file can permanently block subsequent deep-loop acquisition and produce misleading holder metadata.
counterevidenceSought: Checked stale-lock tests; they cover dead-PID and TTL-expired valid JSON locks, but not empty/malformed existing lock files. The CLI status path also maps unreadable locks to exists:false rather than a corrupt state.
alternativeExplanation: Fail-closed on unreadable locks can be intentional for permissions errors, but empty/malformed files from interrupted creation need either typed failure or bounded reclaim, not self-holder substitution.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if lock files are proven impossible to observe between create and JSON write on all supported filesystems and corrupt files are intentionally manual-recovery only with documented operator guidance.
suggestedFix: Return a distinct corrupt-lock state from readLoopLock(), use file mtime/grace to reclaim crash residue, and report corrupt/unreadable holder metadata instead of the candidate lock.

title: Malformed lock files are never reclaimed
detail: readLoopLock() returns null for unreadable or unparsable lock files, and acquireLoopLockFileOnly() then follows the no-holder branch. Because the file still exists, writeLoopLockExclusive() returns false on EEXIST and the function returns acquired:false with the candidate lock as holder instead of aging out or reclaiming the corrupt file. A crash between exclusive open and writing the JSON body can leave this empty/partial lock and block future loop acquisition.
claim: An empty or malformed .deep-loop.lock can permanently block subsequent loop acquisition.
counterevidenceSought: Checked lock tests for malformed or empty lock recovery; coverage exists for valid live holders, valid dead-PID holders, and TTL-expired holders, but not unparsable lock files.
alternativeExplanation: The implementation may intentionally fail closed for unparsable locks to avoid deleting an unknown file.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if the lock contract explicitly requires malformed lock files to block forever until manual operator deletion.
suggestedFix: Distinguish missing lock files from present-but-unparsable files; apply mtime plus a grace/TTL window to corrupt locks, then reclaim through the same rename-and-exclusive-create path used for valid stale holders.

title: Corrupt existing lock files are neither reclaimable nor reported accurately
detail: `readLoopLock()` collapses read/parse failure to null. `acquireLoopLockFileOnly()` treats that as no holder, attempts the fresh exclusive-create path, receives EEXIST from the still-present corrupt file, and returns acquired=false with the attempted new lock as holder. Because stale reclaim only runs after a parsed holder exists, empty or malformed lock files left by a crash have no recovery path. The tests cover fresh contention, live holder refusal, TTL/dead-owner stale checks, and dead-pid reclaim, but not empty, partial JSON, or non-JSON lock files.
claim: A crash or partial write that leaves an empty/malformed lock file can permanently block acquisition instead of being reclaimed or surfaced as a corrupt lock.
counterevidenceSought: Checked loop-lock library and CLI tests for empty, half-written, non-JSON, or unreadable existing lock files; found only fresh contention, normal heartbeat/release, TTL/dead-owner stale checks, and dead-pid reclaim coverage.
alternativeExplanation: This could be intentional fail-closed behavior for corrupt locks, but the returned holder/status is then still misleading and there is no tested operator recovery signal.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if the lock contract explicitly requires malformed lock files to block forever until manual deletion and both library/CLI tests pin that fail-closed behavior with accurate holder/status reporting.
suggestedFix: Distinguish missing from unparsable existing locks. Reclaim or quarantine malformed files after an mtime grace window using the same rename-plus-exclusive-create discipline as stale reclaim, or return an explicit corrupt-lock error with path and recovery instructions. Add Vitest cases for empty, partial JSON, and non-JSON lock files.

title: Review iteration template omits a field the validator requires
detail: The validator expands review iteration requirements to REVIEW_ITERATION_FIELDS when filesReviewed/findingsSummary are required; that list includes findingDetails and the validator rejects rows where findingDetails is not an array. The coupled leaf prompt canonical record and delta example omit findingDetails, so a normal agent following the template can emit a state-log row that fails post-dispatch validation.
claim: The deep-review leaf iteration output contract and post-dispatch validator disagree on whether findingDetails is required.
counterevidenceSought: Checked the command YAML and state JSONL reference; both require findingDetails, so the counterevidence supports validator-side intent but confirms prompt-pack drift.
alternativeExplanation: A runtime prompt assembly layer may add findingDetails outside the inspected template; if so, the template examples are still stale but the normal dispatch path may avoid the failure.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade to P2 if the dispatcher is shown to always inject findingDetails before validation, or if the prompt pack is no longer used by review leaf agents.
suggestedFix: Update the prompt pack canonical JSON and delta examples to include findingDetails: [] for no-finding iterations, or relax validator/state_jsonl/prompt-pack together if findingDetails is intentionally optional.

title: Review prompt can produce JSONL that the validator rejects
detail: The validator requires findingDetails for review-like iteration records, and the review YAML includes findingDetails in assert_jsonl_fields, but the leaf prompt's canonical JSONL schema and example omit findingDetails. A leaf agent following the prompt can therefore emit a state-log record that validateIterationOutputs rejects with jsonl_missing_fields.
claim: A prompt-compliant deep-review iteration can be rejected by the runtime validator because the producer contract and validator contract disagree on findingDetails.
counterevidenceSought: Checked the review state documentation and YAML dispatch contract; the state doc also lists findingDetails as required, so the prompt template is the divergent producer surface.
alternativeExplanation: If another command layer always injects findingDetails into the leaf's state-log record before validation, the trigger would not occur; I found no such injection in the inspected dispatch path before validateIterationOutputs.
downgradeTrigger: Show that every review dispatch path rewrites the leaf JSONL record to include findingDetails: [] before validateIterationOutputs runs, or that assert_jsonl_fields no longer includes findingDetails.
suggestedFix: Update the prompt template and delta examples to include findingDetails: [] for no findings, then add a regression test that validates the prompt example against validateIterationOutputs.

title: Immediate judge rejection leaves fast-timeout timer alive
detail: callJudgeWithDualTimeouts starts invoke(), schedules fastTimer, and awaits the first Promise.race. clearTimeout(fastTimer) only runs after that race fulfills, so an immediate invoke rejection jumps to runJudgeWithHardening's catch path while the fast timer remains scheduled until fastTimeoutMs, which defaults to 10000ms. Repeated fast model errors can keep the event loop open and accumulate idle timers.
claim: Immediate model errors leak the fast timeout timer until it fires.
counterevidenceSought: Checked for a finally block or rejection cleanup around the first race and for tests asserting no pending timers after a model_error retry. The cleanup exists only after a fulfilled race, and the tests cover retry/slow-path behavior without timer-leak assertions.
alternativeExplanation: Short test timeouts can mask the leak because the timer eventually fires; production defaults make the retained timer observable as process tail latency.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if every invoke implementation is proven never to reject after fastTimer is scheduled, or if callers always tear down the process before pending timers can retain the event loop.
suggestedFix: Wrap the first Promise.race in try/finally and clear fastTimer in the finally path on both fulfillment and rejection; preserve the existing slow-path operation.catch suppression for operations that outlive slowTimeoutMs.

title: Post-dispatch validation can fail a valid new iteration when an older JSONL line is malformed
detail: validateIterationOutputs() repairs only a corrupt tail, validates the newest record, then calls stampIterationLogRegion(). That helper reparses every non-empty line in the whole state log without catching JSON.parse failures. If deep-review-state.jsonl has a malformed historical line followed by a valid current iteration, repairJsonlTail() keeps the valid tail and findLastJsonlObjectRegion() throws on the older bad line, causing validateIterationOutputs() to return jsonl_parse_error instead of accepting the valid dispatch. This violates the deep-review loop contract that malformed JSONL is skipped and reconstructed from valid entries.
claim: A non-tail malformed JSONL line can make post-dispatch validation reject an otherwise valid appended iteration record.
counterevidenceSought: Checked jsonl-repair and post-dispatch tests for middle-line corruption coverage; the visible coverage repairs corrupt trailing lines but does not cover a malformed historical line followed by a valid latest iteration.
alternativeExplanation: Post-dispatch validation might intentionally be stricter than reducer replay, but the loop-level recovery contract says malformed JSONL in the loop is skipped and reconstructed from valid entries, so rejecting a valid current append on older corruption is contract drift.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if an upstream invariant guarantees state logs can only corrupt at the tail before post-dispatch validation, or if the intended contract is amended so post-dispatch validation must fail on any historical malformed line.
suggestedFix: Change findLastJsonlObjectRegion() to catch JSON.parse errors and skip malformed non-empty lines while preserving byte offsets for valid records; optionally surface a corruption advisory. Add a regression with a malformed middle line plus a valid latest iteration and assert validation stays ok and stamps logOffset/logSize/logPath.

title: Fan-out review subprocesses get repo-wide write power while isolation is only prompt-enforced
detail: Default fan-out CLI lineages are intended to write only under lineageDir, but missing sandboxMode resolves to workspace-write for Codex and acceptEdits for Claude, while OpenCode is launched with --dangerously-skip-permissions. The implementation states the lineage-dir boundary is enforced by prompt text. A prompt is not a security boundary, so a buggy or prompt-injected child can modify reviewed files or shared state outside the lineage artifact directory during normal fan-out review.
claim: Fan-out CLI lineages can write outside their intended artifact directory by default.
counterevidenceSought: Looked for path-scoped sandboxing, chroot/worktree isolation, or a caller-enforced default sandboxMode; found default write sandbox and prompt-only boundary in the command path.
alternativeExplanation: The comment may reflect an accepted product trade-off because current CLIs lack path-scoped write sandboxes, but that still leaves the advertised lineage boundary unenforced.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if every production fan-out caller always supplies a true path-limited sandbox or isolated worktree outside this module before fanout-run.cjs executes.
suggestedFix: Fail closed for review/context fan-out unless the executor can be constrained to the lineage artifact directory; otherwise run children in a temporary worktree rooted at lineageDir, pass a sandbox that cannot write the source repo, and copy only validated artifacts back.

title: Same-kind fanout replicas still share real CLI homes and lockfiles
detail: fanout-run creates per-lineage .executor-state directories and passes SPECKIT_<KIND>_STATE_DIR, but its own comment says native CLIs read CODEX_HOME, OPENCODE_HOME, or Claude home envs instead. buildExecutorDispatchEnv preserves parent CODEX_, OPENCODE_, and CLAUDE_ environment keys, so same-kind replicas can still share real CLI state and lockfiles under normal count>1 fanout. The tests assert distinct SPECKIT_CODEX_STATE_DIR values, not distinct effective CLI homes.
claim: Same-kind cli-codex, cli-opencode, or cli-claude-code fanout replicas are not effectively state-isolated because the script sets only SPECKIT hints while preserving the real CLI home env vars the children use.
counterevidenceSought: Checked the lineage label schema, state-dir assignment, child environment filtering, and same-kind replica tests. Labels are directory-safe and SPECKIT state dirs are unique, but no inspected code changes the effective CLI homes or serializes same-kind replicas when those homes remain shared.
alternativeExplanation: If current supported CLI builds actually honor SPECKIT_<KIND>_STATE_DIR for lockfiles and session state despite the in-code comment, then the isolation works and the defect collapses to stale documentation/tests.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if integration evidence shows codex/opencode/claude use the SPECKIT_<KIND>_STATE_DIR variables for their lockfiles, or if production fanout prevents concurrent same-kind replicas.
suggestedFix: Either give each spawned CLI an effective per-lineage home/state root that the CLI actually honors, or serialize same-kind replicas behind a per-kind lock when home relocation is not safe. Add a regression that checks effective CODEX_HOME/OPENCODE_HOME/Claude home isolation or proves same-kind concurrent dispatch is blocked.

title: cli-claude-code configDir accepts arbitrary traversal/absolute paths into CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR
detail: configDir is validated only as a non-empty string. fanout-run expands ~ and ~/ but otherwise passes the value unchanged into CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR. A fanout config such as ../../somewhere or /tmp/attacker-claude passes validation and steers the spawned Claude process at an arbitrary config root, which can switch credentials/account state or load attacker-controlled config.
claim: A cli-claude-code lineage can supply a traversal or absolute configDir that is exported directly as CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR for the child process without containment checks.
counterevidenceSought: Checked executor schema, configDir tests, and fanout-run env construction. configDir is restricted to cli-claude-code and passed via environment rather than shell, but I found no traversal, absolute-path, null-byte, or allowlist validation.
alternativeExplanation: Arbitrary config roots may be an intentional operator escape hatch for multi-account testing; in that case this should be documented as trusted input rather than treated as a safe fanout config field.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if fanout configs are explicitly trusted operator-only inputs and arbitrary Claude config roots are a documented supported feature.
suggestedFix: Restrict configDir to a safe home-relative account directory pattern, or resolve it and require it to stay inside HOME or an explicit allowlist. Reject .., null bytes, and absolute escapes. Add negative tests for traversal and absolute paths.

title: Exit-zero salvage misses are reported as fulfilled lineages
detail: fanout-run records the salvage result but rejects only on timeout or non-zero subprocess exit. If a child exits 0 while required iteration markdown is missing and stdout is unrecoverable, fanout-salvage increments failed and writes a failed marker, yet fanout-run returns a fulfilled result. The failure classifier already has salvage_miss semantics, but they are only reached after a throw.
claim: A lineage with unrecoverable missing iteration artifacts can be counted as successful when the subprocess exits 0, silently losing required review output.
counterevidenceSought: Checked fanout-run for any post-salvage guard on salvage.failed and checked the pool classifier/tests for salvage_miss handling; the classifier exists but is only used for rejected worker errors.
alternativeExplanation: The placeholder file may have been intended to let downstream stuck recovery proceed, but the deep-review contract requires dispatch scripts to fail when required iteration artifacts are missing, and fanout-run's summary currently reports the lineage as fulfilled.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if the workflow intentionally treats exit-zero salvage placeholders as valid successful lineage output and a later mandatory gate fails the run before merge/synthesis.
suggestedFix: After runSalvageSweep, throw a retryable lineage error when salvage.failed > 0, attach the salvage summary, and add an exit-zero/unrecoverable-salvage regression test.

title: baseArtifactDir can relocate fanout artifacts outside the canonical review tree
detail: baseArtifactDir rejects only literal .. segments. Absolute paths without .. pass, and the script derives lineagesDir, ledger, summary, checkpoint, state dirs, and log writes from that value. buildLoopPrompt then tells child agents to write all outputs to the resulting lineage directory, while the opencode branch runs with --dangerously-skip-permissions. A malformed or injected base artifact path can move fanout state and child writes outside the expected packet/artifact root.
claim: A baseArtifactDir such as /tmp/outside-root passes validation and becomes the root for fanout state plus child-agent output instructions, so the script lacks a containment guard for artifact root resolution.
counterevidenceSought: Checked for a later path.resolve/path.relative containment check, label-based escape, and shell interpolation. Labels are safe and there is no shell injection, but no runtime guard keeps baseArtifactDir under a canonical artifact root after resolution.
alternativeExplanation: If baseArtifactDir is a trusted host-only parameter and arbitrary absolute artifact roots are intentionally supported, the code is behaving as designed, but that trust boundary is not enforced by the script.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if callers prove baseArtifactDir is never derived from user/configurable input and external absolute artifact roots are documented as supported for all spawned CLI modes.
suggestedFix: Resolve baseArtifactDir to an absolute path and require it to stay under the canonical artifact root for the resolved spec folder or another explicit allowlisted root. Reject null bytes, traversal, absolute escapes, and symlink escapes before creating directories or spawning child CLIs.

title: Reducer can drop findings when the delta file lacks per-finding rows
detail: reduce-state builds findings from separate type=finding delta rows and markdown parsing, while post-dispatch validation only requires findingDetails to be an array and the delta file to contain an iteration row. A nonzero-finding iteration with findingDetails in the canonical record but no per-finding delta lines can validate and then reduce to an empty openFindings registry.
claim: The validator/reducer contract allows structured finding data to pass validation but be ignored by the registry builder, which can produce a false PASS or undercount active findings.
counterevidenceSought: Looked for validation that delta type=finding rows match findingsCount/findingDetails and for reducer fallback from iteration findingDetails; only array-shape validation and iteration-record presence were found.
alternativeExplanation: Agents are instructed to emit per-finding delta rows, so well-behaved iterations avoid the bug; the defect is that the machine gate does not enforce that contract and the reducer ignores the redundant structured data it already validates.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if validateIterationOutputs has another active path that compares findingDetails/findingsCount to type=finding rows before reduce-state runs, or if generated agents cannot produce a delta file without those rows.
suggestedFix: Make post-dispatch validation fail when findingsCount or findingDetails is nonzero but matching type=finding rows are missing, or make reduce-state convert findingDetails into findings as a structured fallback.

title: Model-benchmark runs write reports without the reducer ledger row
detail: The non-reviewer model-benchmark command invokes loop-host without --state-log while using an outputs dir outside any improvement/ ancestor. run-benchmark.cjs only appends benchmark_run when args['state-log'] is provided or inferStateLogPath(outputsDir) finds an ancestor named improvement, so normal runs can produce report.json but leave agent-improvement-state.jsonl without the row reduce-state consumes.
claim: Normal non-reviewer /deep:model-benchmark runs can silently omit the benchmark_run ledger record, causing reduce-state to undercount benchmark runs and stop-status inputs.
counterevidenceSought: I checked the loop-host option forwarding and the model-benchmark YAML commands. loop-host forwards state-log only if it receives one, and the non-reviewer YAML branches do not pass it; only the reviewer-scorer branch includes an explicit --state-log.
alternativeExplanation: A human verification action could catch the missing row, but the configured command itself still omits the path and auto mode has no executable fail-closed guard in this snippet.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if the command renderer injects --state-log into the loop-host invocation outside the YAML text shown here, or if reduce-state no longer consumes agent-improvement-state.jsonl for benchmark accounting.
suggestedFix: Pass --state-log {spec_folder}/improvement/agent-improvement-state.jsonl through both model-benchmark loop-host commands, or make loop-host derive and require the ledger path for model-benchmark mode.

title: Continuation reservation failure leaves the session locked in memory
detail: maybeContinueGoal() adds the session to runtimeState.inFlightContinuations before reserveContinuationTurn(), but cleanup only happens after that reservation returns or inside the later promptAsync finally. If the queued state read/write throws during reservation, the function exits before deleting the lock. Subsequent idle handling sees the stale lock and suppresses the session as continuation_in_flight; the plugin event wrapper catches and swallows the original error.
claim: A transient goal-state write failure during active-continuation reservation can permanently suppress continuation for that session until the plugin process restarts.
counterevidenceSought: Checked the continuation and lifecycle tests for reservation-write failure or stale-lock recovery. They cover default-off, smoke, successful active prompting, prompt blocks, and auto-turn caps, but not a thrown reserveContinuationTurn() path. I also reproduced the failure with a temp state directory made unwritable after goal creation: maybeContinueGoal() rejected and the runtimeState lock remained set.
alternativeExplanation: If OpenCode rebuilt the plugin runtimeState for every event, the stale lock would not survive. In this module, runtimeState is allocated once in MkGoalPlugin() and captured by the event handler, so it persists for the plugin instance.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if OpenCode guarantees a fresh plugin instance per event, or if the filesystem/state-write path cannot throw after inFlightContinuations.add() succeeds.
suggestedFix: Put the reservation call and promptAsync call under one try/finally after adding inFlightContinuations, or move volatile lock acquisition after a successful durable reservation while preserving compare-safe reservation semantics.

title: stateDir override can escape the goal state root
detail: normalizeOptions accepts rawOptions.stateDir, trims it, and passes it through path.resolve without checking that the resolved directory remains under the intended default store. ensureGoalStateDir then creates that directory, and writeGoalAtomic writes the session JSON beneath it. The session id itself is hex-keyed before it becomes a filename, but that does not constrain the parent directory. A plugin registration/config path that passes stateDir like ../../outside-goal-state can move subsequent goal writes outside .opencode/skills/.goal-state.
claim: The plugin prevents session-id path traversal via hex filename keying, but still allows state-dir traversal because the parent state directory override is resolved without an allowlist or base-directory check.
counterevidenceSought: Checked whether the command markdown exposes stateDir, whether tests reject traversal, and whether coupled docs mark stateDir as test-only or trusted-only. The command does not expose it, but no production guard or documented trusted-only restriction was found; tests only exercise trusted tmpdir overrides.
alternativeExplanation: If rawOptions.stateDir is guaranteed to be test-only or otherwise trusted admin-only input in production, this is a hardening/test-gap issue rather than an exploitable traversal.
downgradeTrigger: Prove stateDir cannot be supplied by runtime/plugin configuration in production, or document it as trusted-only and add regression tests that cover rejected traversal attempts.
suggestedFix: Resolve state directories through a dedicated helper that rejects escaped relative paths unless an explicit test-only/trusted override is enabled. Add regression coverage for ../ traversal attempts while preserving tmpdir-based tests through that explicit override.

title: Fan-out timeout and stop paths do not reliably terminate lineage subprocesses
detail: runLineageProcess marks a timeout and sends SIGTERM, but it resolves only when the child emits close; there is no grace timer, SIGKILL escalation, process-group kill, or fallback resolution. The external SIGINT/SIGTERM handler also exits immediately after sending one signal to active children and writing a partial summary, so a child that ignores or delays SIGTERM can keep writing after the parent reports stopped.
claim: Fan-out lineages are not reliably terminated on timeout or parent signal, so normal timeout/interrupt handling can hang the runner or leave orphan writers racing later merge/resume steps.
counterevidenceSought: Checked fanout-run.cjs for a second kill timer, SIGKILL, process-group kill, or a close-wait in the signal handler; none is present. The sibling executor-audit.ts path does implement detached process groups plus SIGTERM-to-SIGKILL escalation, but fanout-run does not use it.
alternativeExplanation: Supported CLI executors may usually exit promptly on SIGTERM, but the code does not enforce that assumption and does not handle descendants or delayed signal handling.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if fanout-run is only invoked under an outer supervisor that kills the entire process tree and never relies on runLineageProcess timeout completion or stopped summaries.
suggestedFix: Run lineage subprocesses in a killable process group where supported, add a timeout grace period that escalates SIGTERM to SIGKILL, and make the signal-stop path wait for child close or record explicit forced-kill/orphan state before exiting.

title: Pre-mutation mirror sync blocks legitimate agent-definition promotion
detail: For agent-definition targets, promote-candidate.cjs reads the candidate content and runs verifyMirrorSync before the phase-specific copy. verifyMirrorSync compares that candidate body against the current .opencode/.claude/.codex agent mirrors, including the target path itself. In a normal accept/ship or legacy promote where the candidate differs from the current target, the mirror gate rejects the change before the accepted snapshot is copied.
claim: Agent-definition promotion can fail before mutation even when the candidate is valid, because the all-mirrors-in-sync check is performed against the future candidate before any mirror has been updated.
counterevidenceSought: I checked the test coverage for accept/ship; it uses temporary non-agent target files, so it does not exercise isAgentDefinitionTarget or the mirror-sync branch.
alternativeExplanation: If the intended workflow requires all mirrors to be pre-updated before promotion, then this helper should not present itself as copying a candidate into a canonical target; as written, the copy happens after the gate that requires the copy's result.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if deep-improvement never promotes .opencode/.claude/.codex agent definitions, or if candidates are always pre-landed into every runtime mirror before promote-candidate.cjs runs.
suggestedFix: Split mirror handling into preflight and post-copy checks: verify current mirrors before mutation if needed, copy/stage the accepted candidate into the target or all mirrors, then run the all-in-sync gate on the resulting files.

title: Workflow promotion still defaults to immediate canonical mutation
detail: promote-candidate.cjs implements accept and ship phases, but normalizePhase defaults a missing --phase to promote and the legacy promote branch still copies candidate over target directly. The normal promotion commands in agent-improvement confirm and model-benchmark auto/confirm omit --phase, so selecting the accepted-candidate option does not create an acceptance file or run the ship precondition path.
claim: The accepted-vs-shipped safety split is bypassed in the normal workflow commands because they invoke the promoter without --phase.
counterevidenceSought: I checked the promotion command strings in the workflow YAML and the promoter's phase dispatch. None of the normal command strings pass --phase=accept or --phase=ship.
alternativeExplanation: The default promote phase may be kept for backward compatibility, but the current workflow text says accepted candidate/variant and does not opt into the new accept/ship contract.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if these YAML commands are not used by the shipped workflows, or if an outer orchestration layer rewrites them into explicit accept and ship invocations before execution.
suggestedFix: Update workflows to create an acceptance file with --phase=accept and ship with --phase=ship --acceptance-file=..., or make --phase mandatory so accidental legacy mutation fails closed.

title: Rollback ignores the stored accepted-state hash before restoring backup
detail: The acceptance state records status and preAcceptTargetHash, but rollback-candidate.cjs does not check either before copying acceptedState.preAcceptBackupPath over the canonical target. A stale or modified backup file under the archive path will be restored as if it were the verified pre-acceptance target.
claim: Rollback can silently corrupt the canonical target if the accepted-state backup file is modified, because the stored hash is available but unused.
counterevidenceSought: I checked rollback-candidate.cjs for any sha256 verification or acceptedState.status guard and found only path existence plus manifest/config target checks.
alternativeExplanation: The archive directory may be treated as trusted, but this is a recovery path and the acceptance file already records the exact hash needed to verify the backup before mutation.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if archive backups are immutable by construction in the runtime environment, or if rollback is never invoked from acceptance files.
suggestedFix: When --acceptance-file is used, require acceptedState.status === 'accepted' and verify sha256(backup) equals acceptedState.preAcceptTargetHash before copying; fail closed on mismatch.

title: Command router behavior is only manually covered
detail: goal.md defines the user-facing routing grammar, but the automated tests start at plugin.tool.mk_goal.execute or executeGoalAction and never exercise the command markdown route selection. The manual playbook contains live /goal steps, so command drift, allowed-tool drift, and router-level failure text drift are not guarded by the plugin test suite.
claim: The plugin suite does not cover the /goal command-router contract even though the command is part of the integrated goal surface.
counterevidenceSought: Searched .opencode/plugins, .opencode/commands, .opencode/bin, and .opencode/scripts for goal.md or /goal command tests; found direct tool tests and manual playbook coverage but no automated command-router harness.
alternativeExplanation: Because OpenCode commands are markdown instructions, the team may accept manual live validation for command routing; that still leaves a traceability gap relative to automated plugin suite coverage.
downgradeTrigger: Drop once an automated command-router contract test or live command smoke pins empty/show/set/bare text/clear/complete/pause/failure routing.
suggestedFix: Add a command-router contract harness that feeds representative $ARGUMENTS cases and asserts the selected mk_goal or mk_goal_status call plus the expected STATUS envelope/failure text.

title: Unsupported-verb contract conflicts with bare-text routing
detail: The command contract promises unsupported verbs emit STATUS=FAIL, but the execution order routes every other non-empty QUERY to mk_goal set. That makes command-like typos such as /goal delete create a goal objective instead of failing as documented.
claim: The command markdown has a router contract drift that can surprise users and tests because unsupported verbs and bare objectives are not distinguishable under the current rules.
counterevidenceSought: I checked whether goal.md defines a reserved verb list or a syntax rule for distinguishing bare objectives from unsupported verbs; it does not.
alternativeExplanation: The intended behavior may be that all unknown text is an objective and line 30 is stale documentation.
downgradeTrigger: Drop if line 30 is removed or the command intentionally documents unknown tokens as objective text with no unsupported-verb failure mode.
suggestedFix: Either remove the unsupported-verb failure promise, or add explicit reserved-verb handling so unknown command-like verbs fail while ordinary bare objectives still set the goal.

title: Unsupported-verb and bare-text routing contracts conflict
detail: The router contract says unsupported verbs emit `STATUS=FAIL ERROR="unknown action: <verb>"`, but the execution order routes any other non-empty `QUERY` to `mk_goal({ action: "set", objective: QUERY })`. Because bare text is also documented as a set shortcut, a token such as `resume` or a verb typo has two incompatible meanings: fail as unsupported or silently replace the current objective.
claim: The command router gives incompatible instructions for unknown first tokens.
counterevidenceSought: Looked for a command parser test or documented verb namespace that distinguishes unsupported verbs from bare-text objectives; the existing goal tests start at the plugin tool path and do not exercise command argument routing.
alternativeExplanation: The unsupported-verb line may be leftover prose and bare-text set may be intended; that is still a command-contract bug because the markdown is executable routing guidance.
downgradeTrigger: Drop once the unsupported-verb clause is removed, or once command tests define and pin a deterministic parser rule for unknown first tokens.
suggestedFix: Pick one grammar: remove the unsupported-verb failure clause if bare text is intentional, or require explicit `set <objective>` and reserve all first tokens for verbs if unsupported verbs must fail.

title: Continuation budget-exhaustion gate lacks direct regression coverage
detail: The continuation suite verifies active prompt dispatch and the auto-turn cap, while lifecycle tests verify budget_limited after message accounting. It does not exercise the continuation-only budget gate where an active stored goal already has tokensUsed >= tokenBudget before promptAsync dispatch. That gate is the final fail-closed check before autonomous continuation.
claim: A safety-critical budget-exhaustion branch can regress without a test that proves promptAsync is not called and the goal is marked budget_limited.
counterevidenceSought: Checked continuation and lifecycle tests; lifecycle covers budget crossing from message events, but continuation covers default-off, passive, smoke, active dispatch, and cap behavior without an exhausted-budget active-mode case.
alternativeExplanation: The lifecycle budget test plus code review may be considered enough if continuation budget exhaustion is treated as a duplicate defense rather than a primary safety gate.
downgradeTrigger: Drop if a separate active-continuation test already covers exhausted tokensUsed/tokenBudget before promptAsync, or if the budget gate is removed from the continuation safety contract.
suggestedFix: Add an active-mode test with an exhausted active goal, a promptAsync stub that fails if called, and assertions for decision=suppressed, reason=budget_exhausted, status=budget_limited, and continuationSuppressed=true.

title: Continuation gate tests cover the main ladder but not several fail-closed gates
detail: maybeContinueGoal has gates for missing session, active goal presence, suppressed goals, in-flight continuations, permission/question blocks, busy/retry runtime state, cooldown, wall/time caps, budget exhaustion, missing promptAsync, smoke mode, active dispatch, and promptAsync failure cleanup. The continuation test covers default disabled, passive, smoke, active success, and auto-turn cap, but not many fail-closed gates or rejected promptAsync cleanup.
claim: The continuation tests do not adequately cover the full fail-closed gate ladder that controls autonomous prompt submission.
counterevidenceSought: Checked mk-goal-continuation.test.cjs for assertions on missing_session_id, goal_not_active, continuation_in_flight, permission_or_question_block, session_busy, session_retry, cooldown, budget_exhausted, prompt_async_unavailable, and prompt_async_failed; those gate reasons are not exercised.
alternativeExplanation: The current tests hit the highest-level autonomy modes and cap behavior, which is valuable smoke coverage, but they do not pin the fail-closed cases most likely to prevent unsafe continuation.
downgradeTrigger: Drop once table-driven continuation tests cover every gate reason and verify locks/counters after promptAsync rejection.
suggestedFix: Extend mk-goal-continuation.test.cjs with negative cases for each gate reason plus a rejected-promptAsync case that verifies inFlightContinuations is cleared and immediate retry is suppressed by cooldown/counter state rather than a leaked lock.

title: Default-export-only regression guard is not automated
detail: The tests prove that a default export exists by importing default.__test, but they do not assert that mk-goal.js has no named exports. The coupled plugin contract says every plugin file must export a default factory, and the feature catalog records a prior live failure where multiple named exports broke OpenCode plugin loading. A future added named export could therefore leave the unit suite green while violating the loader contract.
claim: The plugin suite does not fully cover the default-export-only contract because it lacks a module-shape assertion that rejects named exports.
counterevidenceSought: Searched the goal plugin tests and coupled plugin docs for an automated Object.keys(module) or no-named-exports assertion; only manual/readme guidance and default.__test imports were present.
alternativeExplanation: The project may rely on live OpenCode smoke testing for this loader shape, but the focus here is automated plugin suite adequacy and the documented regression mode is cheap to pin in unit tests.
downgradeTrigger: Drop once the suite asserts the module export keys are exactly ["default"] or an equivalent plugin-loader smoke test fails on named exports.
suggestedFix: Add a module-shape test importing mk-goal.js and asserting Object.keys(pluginModule) is exactly ["default"], then assert the default is a function and carries the __test surface.

title: Tool-level fail-closed missing-session paths are not pinned
detail: The state helper rejects missing session ids and the tool layer converts errors into STATUS=FAIL envelopes, but tests only cover helper-level rejection and successful tool calls with a sessionID. Missing-session tool context is the externally relevant fail-closed path, and it is not asserted for mk_goal or mk_goal_status.
claim: The plugin suite does not prove the public tool handlers fail closed when ToolContext lacks a session id.
counterevidenceSought: Checked goal tests for missing-session ToolContext cases and no-file-created assertions; only helpers.setGoal(null, ...) covers the lower helper path.
alternativeExplanation: The helper rejection may catch most implementation regressions, but it would not catch wrapper changes that fail open, throw raw errors, or format failures incorrectly at the public tool boundary.
downgradeTrigger: Drop once mk_goal and mk_goal_status have negative ToolContext tests asserting STATUS=FAIL, code=MISSING_SESSION_ID, and no temp state files.
suggestedFix: Add tool-level negative tests for mk_goal.execute({ action: "set", objective: "x" }, {}), mk_goal_status.execute({}, {}), and blank/null session variants, with state directory contents checked after each call.

title: Injection tests cover presence, not adversarial sanitization
detail: mk-goal.js contains explicit prompt-injection hardening for active_goal markers, role labels, long fence markers, and ignore/disregard instruction phrases, and renderGoalInjection places sanitized objective/verifier text into the system block. The automated injection test only asserts the active marker appears, while supervisor tests cover secret redaction but not prompt-injection strings. The manual playbook names prompt injection leakage as a failure mode, so adversarial injection coverage is missing from the automated suite.
claim: The plugin suite does not adequately cover injection hardening because it lacks adversarial objective/evidence cases.
counterevidenceSought: Looked for tests containing active_goal markers, system/developer role labels, triple backticks, or ignore/disregard instruction phrases in objectives/evidence; none were present in the goal plugin tests.
alternativeExplanation: Secret redaction coverage proves one sanitization family works, but prompt-injection hardening is a separate contract with different regexes and output expectations.
downgradeTrigger: Drop once tests assert adversarial objective/evidence strings are redacted or neutralized in injection_preview and transformed system output.
suggestedFix: Add injection-focused tests using [active_goal], system:, developer:, triple backticks, and ignore previous instructions in objectives and verifier evidence, then assert raw strings do not survive in renderGoalInjection or the transform output.

title: Invalid mk_goal actions silently fall back to show
detail: executeGoalAction() maps any missing or invalid args.action to show. The exported tool schema declares an enum, so the normal tool path should reject invalid actions before execute, but the callee itself fails open if schema validation is bypassed or changes. That also conflicts with the command contract's stated unsupported-verb failure mode.
claim: A rejected or misspelled action can produce STATUS=OK ACTION=show instead of a failure envelope when executeGoalAction is reached without schema enforcement.
counterevidenceSought: Checked the tool registration and found an enum schema that should block invalid action values in the normal path; no internal invalid-action failure branch exists inside executeGoalAction itself.
alternativeExplanation: The fallback may be intentional for omitted action values, and the schema may be guaranteed to reject invalid non-empty actions before execute is called.
downgradeTrigger: Drop if the OpenCode tool runtime is confirmed to enforce the enum for every execute path and executeGoalAction is kept strictly test-private for schema-valid calls.
suggestedFix: Return STATUS=FAIL for non-empty invalid actions inside executeGoalAction, while preserving omitted-action show behavior only if that is a deliberate API contract.

title: Fuzzy substring scoring lets very short aliases match at the default merge threshold
detail: calculateNameSimilarity() returns 0.85 for any substring match, and the default consolidation threshold is also 0.85. Because this branch runs before the fuzzy-word minimum length gate, very short same-kind names can cluster with unrelated names that merely contain those letters.
claim: Short substring aliases can produce default-threshold consolidation candidates even when the token-level fuzzy guard would have rejected them.
counterevidenceSought: Checked fuzzy-merge tests; they cover cross-kind rejection and a normal long-phrase near duplicate, but not short aliases or substring false positives.
alternativeExplanation: If callers only query with descriptive multi-word names, this remains a robustness gap. The public API accepts arbitrary node names, including short file/symbol aliases.
downgradeTrigger: Close if a caller-side invariant rejects short query names and short candidate names before fuzzy matching.
suggestedFix: Require a minimum substring length and token boundary for substring scoring, or lower substring-only matches below the default threshold. Add tests for two-character aliases and punctuation-heavy names.

title: appendJsonlIfChangedAtomic is single-writer despite append-shaped API
detail: The exported helper is documented as appending a JSONL row, but it reads the whole file and atomically replaces it with old content plus the new row. Without a writer lock or O_APPEND, concurrent callers can lose one append. Current inspected call sites are single-loop telemetry paths, so this is a robustness/test-gap issue rather than an observed normal-use data-loss defect in this slice.
claim: The helper's API/documentation overstates append safety and does not encode its single-writer precondition.
counterevidenceSought: Current rg call sites are single-loop telemetry writes guarded away from fanout, so I did not find a current multi-process caller in this slice.
alternativeExplanation: The helper may be intentionally scoped to single-writer telemetry and named for atomic replacement rather than multi-writer append semantics.
downgradeTrigger: Drop if maintainers mark the helper private/single-writer-only and tests assert that contract, or if a lock/append-only implementation is added before any multi-process reuse.
suggestedFix: Rename/document it as single-writer replace-append, or introduce a lock-held/O_APPEND path before reusing it for multi-process ledgers.

title: appendJsonlIfChangedAtomic can lose rows under concurrent writers
detail: The helper reads the whole current JSONL file, appends the new row in memory, and replaces the file with writeTextAtomic(). Without a writer lock, two independent callers can read the same old content and race their replacement writes, leaving only the last writer's row.
claim: The helper's atomic replacement protects against torn writes but not lost updates, so its name and append contract are unsafe for multi-process ledgers.
counterevidenceSought: Searched usages and tests; production code currently uses mergeJsonlUnderLock() for salvage merging, while appendJsonlIfChangedAtomic() is covered only by serial unit tests.
alternativeExplanation: If this helper is deliberately process-local telemetry, the data-loss risk is a contract/documentation gap rather than an active production bug.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade or close if the helper is marked private/single-process-only and no cross-process caller can import it for shared JSONL files.
suggestedFix: Use a writer lock or delegate to mergeJsonlUnderLock() for shared JSONL files; add a concurrent-writer regression test if the helper remains public.

title: JSONL merge writer omits fsync before rename
detail: writeJsonlRecordsAtomic() writes a temp file and renames it, but it never fsyncs the temp file or parent directory. The shared text writer performs temp write, temp fsync, rename, and parent-directory fsync. A host or power crash after the merge returns can still lose the supposedly committed merged state log.
claim: The JSONL merge path is atomic against torn readers, but it does not provide the crash durability implied by the runtime's temp+fsync+rename state-safety contract.
counterevidenceSought: Looked for an fsync in writeJsonlRecordsAtomic() and in its caller mergeJsonlUnderLock(); the lock protects concurrency but does not add durability.
alternativeExplanation: The intended guarantee may be process-crash safety only, not host/power-loss durability.
downgradeTrigger: Drop if the JSONL merge contract explicitly excludes fsync durability and only promises readers never see partial files.
suggestedFix: Reuse writeTextAtomic() for the JSONL merge payload, or copy its temp fsync plus parent-directory fsync sequence into writeJsonlRecordsAtomic().

title: Judge hardening leaves the fast-timeout timer active on immediate rejection
detail: callJudgeWithDualTimeouts starts a fast-timeout timer for the first Promise.race and clears it only after the race resolves normally. If invoke() rejects before that timer fires, the await throws, runJudgeWithHardening records a model_error, and the timer can keep the Node event loop alive until fastTimeoutMs expires. Existing tests cover retry after rejection but not timer cleanup.
claim: Immediate judge rejection leaves the fast timeout active until it fires, creating a bounded event-loop leak/delay in the judge failure path.
counterevidenceSought: Checked the judge hardening tests; the transient model failure test exercises rejection but does not assert pending timers or process lifetime after the rejection path settles.
alternativeExplanation: A test runner using fake timers or process teardown can hide the leak, but the code path still skips clearTimeout when the first race rejects.
downgradeTrigger: Show that rejected Promise.race branches automatically cancel the timer in this runtime, or add a passing regression that asserts no pending fast timer after immediate invoke rejection.
suggestedFix: Wrap the first Promise.race in try/finally and clear fastTimer in the finally; add a fake-timer regression for immediate rejection cleanup.

title: Fan-out persists raw subprocess stdout for salvage without a redaction pass
detail: fanout-run captures child stdout up to 20 MiB, writes it verbatim to logs/fanout-lineage.out, and fanout-salvage can copy up to 50k recovered text into an iteration markdown file. If a child prints credential-bearing tool output or source content during a failed lineage, the salvage/debug artifacts retain it unredacted.
claim: Fan-out salvage/debug artifacts can persist raw child stdout containing secrets.
counterevidenceSought: Looked for a redaction helper between stdout capture, log write, and salvage write; the code writes savedStdout directly and salvage writes recoveredText directly.
alternativeExplanation: The artifacts are local and intended for recovery/debugging, so the operational exposure may be limited to repo artifact handling.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if lineage stdout is guaranteed by every supported CLI to contain only already-redacted assistant text and these logs are excluded from persistence/sharing.
suggestedFix: Run captured stdout through a shared redaction helper before writing logs or salvaged markdown; persist a bounded raw log only behind an explicit debug flag and outside review artifacts.

title: Pool abort-requeue lag action is not reachable from fanout-run
detail: fanout-pool implements lagCeilingAction=abort-requeue and has tests for it, but fanout-run passes only lagCeilingMs into runCappedPool and the fanout config schema exposes no lagCeilingAction field. The production runner therefore cannot activate the tested abort/requeue path.
claim: The pool API and runner/config contract have drifted: abort-requeue behavior is tested in isolation but dead through the real fanout-run entrypoint.
counterevidenceSought: Checked fanoutConfigSchema and fanout-run's runCappedPool call for a lagCeilingAction passthrough; only lagCeilingMs is wired.
alternativeExplanation: Abort-requeue may be reserved for direct module consumers rather than CLI fanout; if so, the public runner contract should say the CLI only warns on lag ceiling.
downgradeTrigger: Drop if abort-requeue is intentionally private test-only scaffolding or another wrapper passes lagCeilingAction directly to runCappedPool outside fanout-run.
suggestedFix: Either add lagCeilingAction to fanoutConfigSchema and pass it through from fanout-run, or remove/de-scope the abort-requeue branch from production-facing pool tests and docs until wired.

title: Diff-gated JSONL append has no multi-writer coverage despite read-rewrite semantics
detail: `appendJsonlIfChangedAtomic()` reads the whole current file, builds currentContent plus the new row, and replaces the file. That is safe for a single writer but can lose a row if two writers read the same old content and race their replacements. The status-ledger call sites currently appear single-loop-gated, so this is a contract/test gap rather than a demonstrated normal-use data-loss finding. The Vitest coverage calls the helper sequentially in-process with fresh caches.
claim: The tests do not prove whether appendJsonlIfChangedAtomic is single-writer only or safe under concurrent status-ledger writers.
counterevidenceSought: Checked for a cross-process appendJsonlIfChangedAtomic test or a caller-side assertion that the status ledger is single-writer; found sequential helper coverage and YAML call sites that skip fanout but no explicit concurrency contract test.
alternativeExplanation: The helper may be intentionally single-writer because command orchestration serializes the status ledger writes. If so, the missing piece is explicit contract coverage rather than a required locking change.
downgradeTrigger: Drop if the helper is renamed/documented as single-writer and tests assert the relevant command path cannot run parallel writers for the same ledger.
suggestedFix: Either document and type/name this helper as single-writer only, or protect it with a writer lock/O_APPEND-style append strategy. Add a cross-process barrier test with two different rows, or add caller-level tests proving loop-lock/status-ledger single-writer gating.

title: The append-boundary concurrent test is sequential
detail: The test title says it keeps concurrent append records parseable at record boundaries, but it launches the left writer with spawnSync and only starts the right writer after the first process exits. That does not exercise concurrent interleaving. The lock-held merge path does have real Promise.all multi-process coverage, but the bare appendJsonlRecord path is only tested sequentially.
claim: The jsonl-repair Vitest suite claims concurrent append-boundary coverage without actually running concurrent append writers.
counterevidenceSought: Checked the rest of jsonl-repair.vitest.ts for another bare appendJsonlRecord concurrent test; only mergeJsonlUnderLock uses Promise.all with multiple writers.
alternativeExplanation: Bare append may not be intended to support concurrent writers, with mergeJsonlUnderLock being the only supported concurrent path. If so, the test should not claim concurrent append coverage.
downgradeTrigger: Drop if the test is renamed to sequential append coverage and docs state that concurrent JSONL writes must use mergeJsonlUnderLock.
